**Phase 1 complete** — weight 14.2 kg confirmed, classic croup picture with stridor at rest and mild recession. No hard signs of airway emergency. Proceeding to guideline retrieval.

**Differential check clear** — no drooling, no toxic appearance, no choking history, no sudden onset, bilateral clear chest. Epiglottitis, bacterial tracheitis, FB aspiration, and anaphylactic oedema all ruled out by the note's features. Proceeding.

**Severity match: moderate** — stridor at rest, mild suprasternal and intercostal recession, alert and mildly distressed. Maps directly to the moderate row → `croup-dex-moderate`.

---

**Assessment:** Classic viral croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) in a 3-year-old. Severity is **moderate** — rest stridor with mild recession, alert and mildly distressed. Differential is clear; no features to suggest epiglottitis, bacterial tracheitis, foreign body, or anaphylactic oedema.

**Plan:** Single oral dose of dexamethasone as per the dose card above. No nebulised adrenaline indicated at this stage given stable SpO2 and absence of severe recession or agitation.

**Reassessment:** Observe in department and reassess at the window shown on the reassessment card. Watch closely for any increase in work of breathing, escalating agitation, onset of lethargy, or falling saturations — any of these warrants immediate re-evaluation ahead of schedule rather than waiting. Per guideline, a child who presented as moderate croup should **not** be discharged at night if symptoms persist at reassessment; short-stay admission or further observation applies in that scenario.

> ⚠️ **Clinician confirmation required** — please review the dose card and confirm before administering. You remain the prescribing clinician; this is a decision-support output only.

transcripts.prose:
**Phase 1 — Triage complete.** All required data is present: weight 14.2 kg, age 3 years, clear presentation. No missing slots.

**Phase 2 — Diagnosing.** Classic viral croup picture — barky cough, hoarse voice, inspiratory stridor at rest, mild recession, low-grade fever, clear chest, no choking history. No red flags for epiglottitis (no drooling, no tripod, no muffled voice, no toxic appearance, SpO2 97%, alert). Retrieving the NZ guideline now to confirm severity and select the dose rule.

**Phase 3 — Severity matched.** Jack's presentation — inspiratory stridor at rest, mild suprasternal and intercostal recession, alert and mildly distressed, SpO2 97% — maps cleanly to **moderate** on the guideline's severity row. Differential is clear: no drooling, no toxic appearance, no tripod, no choking history, clear bilateral chest, gradual URTI prodrome. Epiglottitis, bacterial tracheitis, foreign body, and anaphylactic oedema are all excluded on current features.

Calculating dose and retrieving the reassessment plan simultaneously now.

---

**Assessment:** Classic moderate viral croup — confident diagnosis, differential is clean. Severity is **moderate**.

**Plan:** Oral dexamethasone, single dose, administered now. Jack can sit with a parent in a calm environment; minimise distressing procedures. Supplemental oxygen only if required for hypoxia. Nebulised adrenaline is not indicated at this point but should be immediately available if he deteriorates.

**Reassessment:** Observe in the department and reassess at the time indicated on the card below. Watch for signs of escalating respiratory distress — increased recession, increasing agitation or emerging lethargy, any drop in saturations, or worsening stridor unresponsive to the steroid. If he is back to mild or settling at reassessment, discharge planning can begin — noting the guideline rail that **moderate croup is not discharged at night**. If he has escalated to severe at reassessment, move to nebulised adrenaline and re-grade.

---

> ✅ **Dose Card — Dexamethasone (Moderate Croup)**
> **Drug:** Dexamethasone | **Route:** Oral | **Frequency:** Single dose
> **Dose:** 2.13 mg | **Max:** 12 mg | **Capped:** No
> **Calculation:** 0.15 mg/kg × 14.2 kg = 2.13 mg
> 📎 *Starship NZ, 2020 — [starship.org.nz/guidelines/croup](https://www.starship.org.nz/guidelines/croup/)*

---

> 🕐 **Reassessment Plan — Moderate Croup**
> **Reassess in:** 120 minutes
>
> **Watch for:**
> - Persistent inspiratory stridor at rest → ongoing moderate
> - Increased work of breathing → possible escalation to severe
> - SpO₂ < 92% → severe, re-route
> - Agitation or lethargy → impending failure
>
> **At reassessment:**
> | Severity | Action |
> |---|---|
> | Mild | Discharge planning (not at night if initial presentation was moderate) |
> | Moderate | Review for a further 2 hours, then discharge or admit |
> | Severe | Nebulised adrenaline + re-treat as severe croup |
>
> ⚠️ *Escalate at any point if clinical state worsens. Moderate or severe croup is not discharged at night.*
>
> 📎 *Starship NZ, 2020 — [starship.org.nz/guidelines/croup](https://www.starship.org.nz/guidelines/croup/)*

---

**Confirm this dose before administration.** If Jack's weight or the working diagnosis changes, let me know and I'll recalculate.

transcripts.prose:
**Phase 1 — Triage complete.** Age 7, weight 25 kg, all key fields present. Reviewing differential before proceeding — no drooling, no tripod, no toxic appearance, clear URTI prodrome. Reassuring against epiglottitis and bacterial tracheitis. Loading NZ croup guideline now.

**Differential check — clean.** No drooling, no tripod, no toxic appearance, clear URTI prodrome → epiglottitis and bacterial tracheitis are not supported. No choking history → foreign body aspiration not supported.

**Severity match:** Persistent stridor at rest, severe recession, agitation, SpO2 91% — maps squarely to the **severe** row. Note the patient is agitated (not obtunded or exhausted), and stridor is prominent rather than diminishing — impending respiratory failure criteria are not met. Proceeding with `croup-dex-severe`.

Dose capped at the ceiling. Now retrieving the reassessment plan.

---

**Assessment:** Severe croup in a 7-year-old, consistent with the Starship NZ severe severity row. Dose is capped at the adult ceiling — Sione's weight-based calculation exceeds the maximum, so the ceiling applies. *(The dose card above shows the validated capped dose — confirm before dispensing.)*

**Plan:** Single oral dose of dexamethasone as per the dose card. Alongside the steroid: supplemental oxygen is indicated given SpO2 of 91%, and nebulised adrenaline should be considered immediately for a child this symptomatic — the steroid onset is delayed and adrenaline provides the acute bronchodilation. Avoid IV access attempts unless absolutely necessary; in a child with severe upper airway obstruction, failed cannulation attempts can precipitate respiratory arrest — if intubation is required, plan for gas induction.

**Reassessment:** The reassessment card above carries the structured plan from the guideline. Key things to track: the number of adrenaline doses given, whether the response to adrenaline is sustained or rebounds (rebound at the reassessment window raises the question of bacterial tracheitis), and SpO2 trajectory. **Differential check — Phase 2:**

Marcus's presentation is atypical in two ways: **age 17** (croup peaks at 6 months–3 years and is uncommon in adolescents) and **no URTI prodrome**. I've cross-checked the guideline's differential against the note:

- **Epiglottitis** — absence of prodrome matches, but Marcus has a *barky* cough (not muffled/hot-potato voice), is afebrile (37.2°C), alert, not drooling, no tripod posturing. Barky cough is strongly against epiglottitis. ✅ Not a blocker.
- **Bacterial tracheitis** — afebrile, not toxic-appearing, no purulent secretions. ✅ Not a blocker.
- **Foreign body aspiration** — no choking history documented, barky cough and hoarseness are laryngeal rather than tracheal. ✅ Not a blocker.
- **Anaphylactic oedema** — no allergen exposure, no urticaria, no wheeze, haemodynamically stable. ✅ Not a blocker.

The barky cough and hoarse voice are highly characteristic of laryngotracheobronchitis and collectively make the must-not-miss alternatives unlikely in a stable, afebrile, non-toxic patient. The atypical age and absent prodrome are worth documenting, but the differential is clean enough to proceed.

**Severity — Phase 3:**

Inspiratory stridor at rest, mild recession, alert — this maps to **moderate** on the guideline's severity table. SpO2 96% is not severe hypoxia. No agitation, no accessory muscle use. → `croup-dex-moderate`.

---

**Assessment:** Moderate viral croup in a 17-year-old — atypical age and absent prodrome noted, but the classical barky cough, hoarse voice, rest stridor, and absence of any toxic features make a viral aetiology the working diagnosis. The dose card and reassessment card are below.

---

**Dose card — dexamethasone**

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Drug** | Dexamethasone |
| **Dose** | **10.2 mg** oral, single dose |
| **Basis** | 68 kg × 0.15 mg/kg = 10.2 mg (under 12 mg cap, uncapped) |
| **Route** | Oral |
| **Frequency** | Single dose |
| **Guideline** | Starship NZ Clinical Guideline, 2020 |
| **Source** | [starship.org.nz/guidelines/croup](https://www.starship.org.nz/guidelines/croup/) |

---

**Reassessment card**

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Reassess at** | 120 minutes post-dose |
| **Watch for** | Persistent stridor at rest · Increased work of breathing · Hypoxia · Agitation or lethargy |
| **If mild at reassessment** | Discharge (not at night for moderate initial presentation) |
| **If still moderate** | Review for a further 2 hours, then discharge or admit |
| **If severe at reassessment** | Nebulised adrenaline + escalate to severe pathway |
| **Universal rail** | Escalate immediately at any point if clinical state worsens · Moderate croup is **not** discharged at night |

---

**Plan:** Single oral dose of dexamethasone via the moderate pathway, then observe in department for the reassessment window. Given the atypical age — worth documenting that you considered and excluded must-not-miss alternatives at the bedside, and consider whether nasoendoscopy or ENT review is warranted if he doesn't respond as expected. A teenager with croup who fails to respond to steroids should prompt active reconsideration of the differential.
